NATION HERE TODAY

have only to break down the bar.
riers the customs of day that is
past have raised against our par
ticipation in what were formerly
regarded as mans affairs. 1t is
the potent silent influences women
should possess that we are depend
ing ON for our success in attain.
ing the confidence and opportunity
that thinking, working. hoping,

I'VE are not working for a posi-
ton Of opposition to men, but sim
ply for one of greater cooperation
for the benefit of the whole world
and not for our sex alone. We
hope by our visit and convention in
this state to do what we can to aid
in the enactment of the equal suf-
frage amendment to the Washing
ton state constitution, and it is our
determination to make this conven-
ton the best we have ever held"


Rev. Shaw had little sleep last
night, but this morning found her
as bright and companionable as a
woman of great capability and
grace of mind and heart can be.


Other officers of the national as
ISOLATION with the party are Mrs.
Rachel Foster Avery, of Philadel.
phia, first vice president and
daughter of the founder of the
Pittsburg ispatch; Miss Florence
Kelly, second vice president, Sec
rotary of the National Consumers
league and daughter of Congress-
man Kelly; Miss Kate Gordon of
New Orleans, correspondent secre-
tary and one of the foremost wom-
en- of the south; Miss Alice Stone
Blackwell of Boston, recording Sec
rotary; Mrs. Harry Taylor Upton
of Ohio, recording secretary and
daughter of Judge Taylor, who
succeeded Garfield in congress
when the latter was elected to the
presidency; Miss Laura Clay of
Kentucky, auditor, and daughter of
Cassius M. Clay; Mrs. Ella Stew
art of Chicago, also an auditor.
Every member of the national
board is with the party. II


Mrs. Carrie Chapman Gait, presi-
dent of the International Suffrage
association, is i'll in a New York
hospital and could not accompany
the delegation.


Mrs. May Arkwright Hutton,
head of the Spokane Equal Suf-
frage association; Mrs. LaReine
Bake Of Spokane, delegate to the
recent international suffrage con-
vention held in London; members
of the Spokane suffrage associa-
